setting.note: Newly widowed, the Senegalese schoolteacher Ramatoulaye writes a long letter to her lifelong friend Aissatou, taking stock of her marriage, her husband's taking of a young second wife, and the choices open to educated women in post-independence Senegal. Mariama Bâ's landmark of African women's writing is set in the real Dakar: the widow's house through the confinement of mourning, the mosque and the rites, the schools where the two women trained as pioneers, and the wider world Aissatou escapes to.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Mariama Bâ, first published 1979 (Une si longue lettre). Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: So Long a Letter, Mariama Bâ (1979). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The deep plum of a woman's private reckoning, set down in the letter she writes through the long months of mourning. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
